item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does this not have an expanded collab testing log? This is gold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it, upvoted, but was put off by:

Additionally, when he replaces a major character, or his presence is otherwise obvious, no characters in the film will take notice of him, regardless of the capacity he is in.

I presume that was meant to indicate that other characters treat him as if he was the original actor/prop/set dressing etc but as phrased it sounds like they ignore his presence completely, which the examples make clear is not the case. Just a thought.

item.title: This needs a testing log
item.content:encoded: Having a catalog of roles Hugo Weaving has starred in following the established format so far would be hilarious! This SCP is begging to have one added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hilarious scp, but it's way too silly to be in the main series. It also needs experiment logs like 914.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Empire is in black and white, so Hugo Weaving being golden when playing the sun seems off. Is that a mistake or is he the only color in the film?

item.title: Hilarious
item.content:encoded: This really should be a -J but I like it too much to downvote it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit of a necro, but….

+ show block
– hide block

Observation Log: 07/30/18

Film Title: Cloud Atlas (2012)

Role Taken: All other actors' clothing

Variations: All scenes which do not include a character played by Hugo Weaving have at least one character "wearing" the actor, with Weaving gripping his fellow actor and/or contorting his body to resemble the article of clothing he is meant to be (e.g. draped over the shoulders of Jim Broadbent as though he were a jacket, wrapped around Halle Berry's neck as a scarf). All remaining articles of clothing are made from what appear to be life-sized cardboard cutouts of Weaving. Scenes often take significantly longer to play out due to the obvious physical strain placed on the actors, though in some cases scenes end abruptly as actors fall or otherwise injure themselves attempting to complete the scene. Despite this, no corresponding injuries are evident on the involved actors throughout the rest of the film.

Notably, Weaving is fully nude while in the "role" of clothing, and the cardboard cutouts used to create other clothing are likewise images of Weaving in the nude. There is no overt acknowledgement of this during the film's plot (other than an example of a scene-ending injury when actor Jim Sturgess falls and [REDACTED]), but a prominent notice placed prior to the beginning of the film warns that "This film is rated NC-17 for nudity throughout."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed so hard at the penguin part, especially the commentary by Morgan Freeman. Love this SCP right now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lovely

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
Gosh Darn it Hugo!!

https://youtu.be/cjxSCYkIgNc

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+Proposal
-Proposal

As a tale depicting the creation of this SCP already exists, we'v decided a crosslink to this would be appropriate. This would be placed in a line directly linked to the tale as shown.

Before changes:

John,
Aldon got your letter. We can definitely work with that. Can't wait to get started. You'll know when we're done.
A&F

After changes:

John,
Aldon got your letter. We can definitely work with that. Can't wait to get started. You'll know when we're done.
A&F

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kaktus, I love this shit, but there's a gigantic missed opportunity here: Being John Malkovich. Hugo Weaving plays John Malkovich, but everyone still calls him John Malkovich. I just think that there's some kind of comedic avenue here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this could use an experiment log, tbh. There are so many movies where this would shine at.

If there was one, this would be my contribution to it.

Film Title: Night at the Museum

Role Taken: The entire museum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logs are hilarious. Hugo Weaving as Prof. X's wheelchair is what pushed me over the edge into real laughter. The SCP is delightfully odd to me, a nonviolent curiosity that still manages to be eerie, coming alive after dark. And is it aware? Is Hugo watching us, too? Is a phantom Hugo forced to live out an infinite number of bizarre cameos in a film prison universe?

Oh, positively +1! The article is not too long or too short, just right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, Agent Smith?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two small, neat things which tipped me over the line into an upvote:

  • the fact that Hugo Weaving movies probably did (at one stage) have a correlation with increased receipts, considering the Matrix and LOTR trilogies came out around the same time;
  • the fact that the first additional head to appear on hydra-Weaving was the Red Skull, the head of Hydra.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to imagine that it triggers a feedback loop and you end up with something like the John Malkovich scene from Being John Malkovich.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, but it would have been easy enough to include Priscilla, Queen of the Desert or something in the examples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He probably appears twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Failure to answer the obvious: "What happens if Weaving is already in the film?" leaves me disappointed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, when combined with related tale, makes for a beautiful (if slightly silly) picture. I'm just sorry Titanic wasn't one that was screened by the Foundation.

This is totally a mainlist article. It is a crafted device with anomalous effect. Scary? Not in a 'my life's in danger' sense. But it definitely attacks the Status Quo. Thus it must be contained; any contained object requires a writeup…

+1. For the (rest of the) penguin sequence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to know what SCP-1781 would do to Cloud Atlas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part with the penguin really made this article. Aside from that, however, I didn't really like it. +1 regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Role Taken: Professor X's wheelchair.

Aaahahahahaha!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for figuring out how to make wishing your favorite actor was in every movie go horribly wrong without killing anyone but a (probably non-existent) penguin. Very bizarre. Personal favorite was him as Professor X's wheelchair. "whirrrrrr…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing. The LaBeouf effect doesn't work on recorded images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if that guy who sees everyone and everything as Shia LeBeouf was to watch a movie at this theater?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You keep making things that I swear will be the last of the thing I upvote. This is the last "thing that makes famous people in stuff" that I'm going to upvote, unless the next one is really, really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what that mysterious reason djkaktus speaks of is, and without it I'm afraid I have to agree. It reads like a really underwhelming note from captain exposition that just zaps all the mystery out of the thing. (I also have a hard time believing that it would actually be true that Hugo Weaving would correlate with increased sales, but that's another issue.)

Oh, and a note:

Individuals who enter the projection booth when SCP-1781 activates seem to experience no passage of time during the film.

Do you mean "Individuals who are in the projection booth when SCP-1781 activates seem to experience no passage of time during the film"? Because nobody can enter it once it activates, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Counter, when commenting on an article, please make an effort to use proper grammar and spelling. I understand that you may not be a native speaker, but that only means you're going to need to watch these subjects even more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is lacking something. After the first read, my initial feeling is the author is a fan of Hugo Weaver, so he tried so hard to merge him to this SCP. While SCP-1247, The Labeouf Viewer, used a similar method, it maintain to be creepy and weird even though you do not know much about Labeouf. However, I do not see anything in this SCP, I think the joke here is too "inside", I doubt those observation Logs will be interesting if you replace Hugo to someone no one like/no one care, downvote for me.

Edit: try to have a better comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it because the creators of the skip are in Anart Antics and Boron Blisters?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great.

I particularly love the note dealing with its origins. I can just imagine someone going about thinking "how can I get this guy in every film" and then picking the most direct route to the solution (even if casting him as a wheel chair was probably not in the original design intent).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Professor X's wheelchair.

I don't even.

Although the penguin egg might actually be the best part of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a reason for the note, one that will become readily apparent here before too long I hope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shia LaBeouf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the observation log and the general premise of the article, but I really dislike the note. It feels tacked on, it's another example of the conveniently-informative-random-note school of SCP writing which I'm gradually getting a bit sick of, it saps all the confusion out of the piece, removes the sense of intrigue, and it's just not a very interesting origin story. I'd much prefer if the reasons for the Weaving were left unexplained, because the confusion of trying to work out why someone somewhere loves Hugo Weaving so much is better than a boring story about executive meddling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great skip but I'm curious what would happen if a movie like "Empire" was played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. Nope nope nope. I strongly disagree. This does not need to be a -J, and probably wouldn't have succeeded if it were. Many main list articles have comedic elements, but work perfectly as regular SCPs because they present the article in a believable way. There's an entirely different set of standards for joke articles, and this doesn't meet them. It's silly, but it's not laugh-out-loud funny from start to finish, nor was it intended to be. It's just an anomaly that creates situations which can humorous, but also highly disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Earlier you stated "Definitely a funny article, and therefore more appropriate as a -J", so I'm not surprised TwistedGears asked his question.

Anyway, we're kind of getting off topic. The focus should be this article; if you'd like to debate whether certain funny SCPs should be -j's, either post on those discussion pages or make a forum thread discussing them in general.

Personally, I think funny mainlist SCPs should stay on the mainlist, because SCP-Js inherently come with the underlying nudge of "no, don't take this seriously, the Foundation doesn't actually have this in containment" whereas with mainlist articles, that's not the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those articles aren't exactly relevant to this one. Just because one article's type of humor is better suited for -J doesn't mean all articles with any humor should also be -J…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So SCP-504, SCP-1171, SCP-1296, SCP-1370, SCP-2006, SCP-2360, SCP-2980, and so on and so on and so on should all be -Js?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. Definitely a funny article, and therefore more appropriate as a -J

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP got it wrong. The theater actually replaces every actor, prop, and background in every movie with Bryan Cranston, Gary Oldman, AND Hugo Weaving. And no one has ever been able to tell the difference between movie showings here and anywhere else, unless you point it out to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hugo Weaving (now considerably more unwieldy under the weight of several heads) is seen stumbling over to Hercules, attempting to eat him and, being unable to do so, resorting to chewing aggressively on his head.

Best line. Too bad including a line like "Hugo Weaving was noted to to make the vocalization "om nom nom" repeatedly before being killed by Hercules" would probably be too silly for a non -J article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I'm getting this right, any film that actually starred Hugo Weaving will not be shown in this movie theater, right?

And why Hugo Weaving? Is he the only actor you managed to shoop over Clark Gable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DISCLAIMER: No penguins were harmed in the making of this film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot upvote anything with such violent cruelty to penguins. My fingers will not let me. D=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, if we're talking about something anomalous that could just be the result of clever special effects, Lebron James might like a word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In-universe, not for the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you, and in response, I made some changes. Swapped out some stuff in the description, expanded on some of the logs, added one and took out another. I think it reads a little better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea all right, but I feel like the observation log didn't sell the article as fully as it could have. There was a lot of potential to push the ideas much further, ramping up the weirdness and interest. Why not have Hydra!Weaving grow extra Weaving heads? Why not have Bone!Weaving act according to the physical properties of a regular bone? Why not have him in the role of a woman? Or as the T-Rex in Jurassic Park? Or as Gandalf's hat? What about animated films? What about theatrical documentaries like Earth? Why not show the consequences of what happens when a film is played that already has Weaving in it? This strikes me as an SCP that is wholly supported by the accompanying log, and right now that log feels too weak to hold up the rest of the article (kinda like the Siskel & Ebert skip before its rewrite). +-0

Also, it was very considerate of the SCP creator to make sure that the backstory-explaining notes regenerate like that. (Also also that is a very, very good photoshop.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm glad you don't hate me :)

Edit: You know, there's also the bit about being unable to access the booth during screenings, and those inside not experiencing the passage of time. That alone, I'd imagine, is enough to warrant some interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't hate you for this, but there's no mystery here. No intrigue, no horror, no questions asked. It's a straightforward "wacky effects" and the two notes explain away any and all potential for mystery.

Basically given the state of CGI today, there is literally nothing to make this actually anomalous, rather than the effort of a skilled visual effects company.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. Simple concept and the observations logs are funny without being silly. The section when the effect is being clarified though, the tone seems a little off somehow. Maybe it's just the phrase "To clarify" doesn't fit foundation tone. The sentence explaining Weaving can be an actor or prop, etc, might be unnecesary too. Funnier if we're unsure until we read the example with him as professor X's chair.
Up voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also think that it would be better as a -J, for the same reasons. It just seems a bit too silly, although it is a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this should be a -J. This is just too nonsensical, at least in my opinion anyway.

Not that this is a bad article, it made me burst out laughing when I read the list of pictures. But it does not have any level of seriousness to it whatsoever. Even moth helmet had a degree of wonder to it.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of Hugo Weaving carrying Patrick Stewart around is quite hilarious.

I think the object needs a bit more meat, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Crayne, sorry about that!
